tweets.text:
China's economic slowdown is deepening:

China's GDP grew just +4.3% YoY in Q2 2026, the slowest pace since Q4 2022 and below the government's official target range of +4.5% to +5.0%.

This followed +5.0% expansion in Q1, bringing first-half growth of the year to +4.7% YoY.

Furthermore, fixed-asset investment fell -5.7% YoY in the H1, worsening from a -4.1% decline in the first 5 months.

The decline was driven by property investment plunging -18%, the largest drop on record dating back to 1992.

At the same time, retail sales increased +1% YoY in June after a -0.6% decline in May, while auto purchases dropped more than -16%.

Weakness is spreading across the world’s 2nd-largest economy.

tweets.text:
The current bull run is historic:

The S&P 500 is up +95% since the end of 2022, placing the current bull market within the strongest 10% at this stage of the cycle, in data going back to 1928.

By comparison, the top 25% of historical bull markets gained roughly +50% over the same period.

Meanwhile, the median bull market delivered just ~35% after 3.5 years.

The current bull run has remained within the strongest 10% of historical bull markets for 2 years, excluding the March-April 2025 correction.

Since the April 2025 low alone, the S&P 500 has surged +51%.

Market momentum is incredibly strong.

tweets.text:
The richest Americans have never controlled this much wealth:

The wealth of the top 0.00001% of Americans is up to a record ~12% of US national income.

National income measures the total income earned across the economy, including wages, business profits, and investment income.

This percentage has more than quadrupled since the 2008 Financial Crisis.

By comparison, this metric never exceeded 1% between the 1950s and the 1990s.

The surge has been fueled by record gains in the equity market and rising real estate prices.

Asset owners are the only winners.

tweets.text:
🇨🇳 #China Should Focus on More Efficient Computing Power - People’s Daily

➡ China should avoid a rush into oversized computing projects and focus instead on improving how resources are used, arguing that efficiency and practical applications matter more than sheer scale, People’s Daily says in a commentary.

➡ Bigger computing capacity doesn’t automatically mean better results and efficient allocation is what boosts productivity.

➡ AI competition is not just about who has more resources, but who can use them more effectively.

➡ Developing new productive forces should not become costly race to build large projects.

➡ Policymakers should focus on smart planning, innovation and real world applications rather than scale.

*Link (Chinese): https://t.co/5pAM9VgppQ

thread_tweets.text:
Appreciate the thesis but don't think it's a big factor. DSX was "open-sourced" May 31 and Mirantis was named as a day-one ecosystem partner...Every Linux needs a Red Hat eg. One week later NVIDIA put $2.1B into IREN.

I think the sector weakness is more straightforward: big liabilities, minimal cash flows, probably bad earnings (delays, weak bitcoin price, higher costs). 

Unwind.
thread_tweets.full_text:
Appreciate the thesis but don't think it's a big factor. DSX was "open-sourced" May 31 and Mirantis was named as a day-one ecosystem partner...Every Linux needs a Red Hat eg. One week later NVIDIA put $2.1B into IREN.

I think the sector weakness is more straightforward: big liabilities, minimal cash flows, probably bad earnings (delays, weak bitcoin price, higher costs). 

Unwind.

tweets.text:
"Most people who need a transplant die without getting one."

Nobel laureate Alvin Roth helped pioneer modern market design — the same toolkit crypto builders use to reason about rules, incentives, and allocation in onchain markets.

Here’s what that thinking looks like when applied to kidneys:

"You can't pay a donor for a kidney. That's a repugnant transaction... Kidneys are gifts. Their price is zero and they're very scarce therefore."

"Maybe I could give a kidney to your kid and you could give a kidney to my kid. That's a kidney exchange. Now we do about a third of living donor transplants through exchange."
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
First Principles Ep. 4 with Alvin Roth

Long before crypto made coordination programmable, Nobel Prize winner Alvin Roth was designing markets where coordination could save lives, matching doctors to hospitals, students to schools, and kidney donors to the patients who need them. https://t.co/qgl7sx4wo9
tweets.full_text:
"Most people who need a transplant die without getting one."

Nobel laureate Alvin Roth helped pioneer modern market design — the same toolkit crypto builders use to reason about rules, incentives, and allocation in onchain markets.

Here’s what that thinking looks like when applied to kidneys:

"You can't pay a donor for a kidney. That's a repugnant transaction... Kidneys are gifts. Their price is zero and they're very scarce therefore."

tweets.text: Hello from #Germany, where bond vigilantes are whispering, not shouting: 10y real yields are pushing toward a 6mth high at 1.08% as Berlin borrows more, while CDS show no alarm: Germany ~7bps vs France ~31bps. Merz says AAA is safe, but he'd “slam on the brakes” if it wasn't. https://t.co/muHTaeeRUM

tweets.text:
Crude’s rally comes with a warning label.

Term structure just crossed above 1.05. Over the past 15 years, crude was higher six months later only 15% of the time.

Risk premiums can fade fast. https://t.co/795mZUInh8

tweets.text: No doubt
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
PREDICTION

after kimi k3 successful launch and mogs opus 4.8, Dario Amodei is gonna write an essay explaining how open-weight Chinese AI threatens democracy, national security, and the future of humanity
tweets.full_text: No doubt

tweets.text: The US does so at its own peril if it continues to dismiss the success of Chinese AI labs as merely the result of distillation.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: Why do you think Chinese labs only got there by distilling alone and lack all other methods? Did you know the top researchers in Chinese labs went to the same top university with similar GPAs as the top researchers in OpenAI/anthropic, which are mostly Chinese as well?
tweets.full_text: The US does so at its own peril if it continues to dismiss the success of Chinese AI labs as merely the result of distillation.

tweets.text: The success of Kimi-K3 once again suggests that the moat surrounding frontier AI labs may be far weaker than many investors believe. The rapid commoditization of AI services—driven by the swift catch-up of Chinese AI labs—is posing an increasingly significant challenge to the valuation narrative underpinning US frontier AI companies.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Big news: Kimi-K3 by @Kimi_Moonshot is now #1 in the Frontend Code Arena with 1679 pts, surpassing Claude Fable 5.

This is a 17-place jump from Kimi-k2.6 (#18 -> #1).

In Frontend, Kimi-K3 ranked #1 in 6 of 7 domains: Brand & Marketing, Reference-Based Design, Data & Analytics, https://t.co/Oa6teaQnWp

tweets.text: @JacobShap I agree that "going at" Argentina would be nutty. But there are ways to do this without playing bunker defense. For example, putting in more players who could "hold the ball" in the midfield when Argentina loses the ball in order to then let some wingers get up the field.
